card_description: Nicolas Malebranche (1638–1715), the French philosopher and Oratorian priest, appears in Dick's Exegesis in connection with his doctrine of 'occasionalism' — the view that God is the only true cause in the universe, and that what we call natural causation is in reality a series of 'occasions' at which God directly intervenes to produce the apparent effects of physical causes. Dick found in Malebranche a philosophical warrant for his sense that the mundane world of apparent cause-and-effect is a secondary reality, a surface phenomenon beneath which divine agency operates directly and continuously. Malebranche's famous formula — 'we see all things in God' (nous voyons toutes choses en Dieu) — resonated with Dick's conviction that genuine knowledge of reality requires direct participation in the divine intellect, not any merely human cognitive capacity. Dick sometimes cites Malebranche in the context of his analysis of what makes certain experiences qualitatively different from ordinary perception: the moments of anamnesis or divine contact that he experienced in 1974 were, on Malebranche's model, moments in which the ordinary veil of secondary causation was stripped away and God's direct agency was directly perceived. Malebranche represents a point of intersection between orthodox Catholic theology and the kind of direct-perception mysticism that Dick associated with the Gnostic and Hermetic traditions, demonstrating to Dick that the Western philosophical mainstream was not wholly closed to the insights that Gnosticism preserved in a more radical form.
full_description:
Nicolas Malebranche (1638–1715), the French philosopher and Oratorian priest, appears in Dick's Exegesis in connection with his doctrine of 'occasionalism' — the view that God is the only true cause in the universe, and that what we call natural causation is in reality a series of 'occasions' at which God directly intervenes to produce the apparent effects of physical causes. Dick found in Malebranche a philosophical warrant for his sense that the mundane world of apparent cause-and-effect is a secondary reality, a surface phenomenon beneath which divine agency operates directly and continuously. Malebranche's famous formula — 'we see all things in God' (nous voyons toutes choses en Dieu) — resonated with Dick's conviction that genuine knowledge of reality requires direct participation in the divine intellect, not any merely human cognitive capacity. Dick sometimes cites Malebranche in the context of his analysis of what makes certain experiences qualitatively different from ordinary perception: the moments of anamnesis or divine contact that he experienced in 1974 were, on Malebranche's model, moments in which the ordinary veil of secondary causation was stripped away and God's direct agency was directly perceived. Malebranche represents a point of intersection between orthodox Catholic theology and the kind of direct-perception mysticism that Dick associated with the Gnostic and Hermetic traditions, demonstrating to Dick that the Western philosophical mainstream was not wholly closed to the insights that Gnosticism preserved in a more radical form.
